---
title: "Git Activity"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
---

```{r}
#| include: false
framework::scaffold()
```

# Git and GitHub Activity

## Goal

Complete one full local-to-remote Git workflow by hand.

## What You Will Do

1. Create a new project folder.
2. Create a file.
3. Initialize a Git repository.
4. Add a remote GitHub repository.
5. Stage and commit a file.
6. Push to GitHub.
7. Create and test a `.gitignore`.
8. Clone the repository into a second directory.
9. Make a change there.
10. Push the change and pull it back into the original directory.

## Before You Start

You will need:

1. Git installed on your machine.
2. A GitHub account.
3. A new empty repository on GitHub.

For practice in class, we will use a public repository.

In real research workflows, you will often use private repositories instead, especially when the project itself is sensitive.

Use your own repository URL anywhere you see `[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]` below.

Use your own folder name anywhere you see `[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]` below.

Do not copy and paste these commands exactly as written. Typing things out yourself will help you understand what you are doing.

I use terminal commands here, but you can use your editor to create directories and files if you prefer.

## Step 1: Create a Project Folder

Terminal:

```bash
mkdir [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
cd [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
```

You can also just use your editor.

## Step 2: Create a File

In your editor, create a new file, such as `README.md`.

Type something and save it.

## Step 3: Initialize Git

```bash
git init
git status
```

Check that Git now sees `README.md` as an untracked file.

## Step 4: Create a Repository on GitHub

In your browser:

1. Log in to GitHub.
2. Go to `Repositories` and click the green `New` button.
4. Enter `[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]` as the repository name.
5. For this class exercise, make it `Public`.
6. Do not initialize the repository with a README, `.gitignore`, or license.
7. Click `Create repository`.

## Step 5: Connect Your Local Repository to GitHub

After GitHub creates the empty repository, run:

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]/[YOUR-PROJECT-
NAME].git
```

You can verify the remote with:

```bash
git remote -v
```

## Step 6: Stage and Commit

```bash
git add README.md
git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

## Step 7: Push to GitHub

```bash
git push -u origin main
```

Here, `-u` means `--set-upstream`.

That tells Git to remember that your local `main` branch is connected to `origin/main`.

After that, you can usually just run:

```bash
git push
git pull
```

## Step 8: Create `.gitignore`

In your editor, create a new file named `.gitignore`.

Add this line to `.gitignore`:

```text
data/
```

## Step 9: Test `.gitignore`

In the terminal:

```bash
mkdir data
git status
```

In your editor or file browser, create a file named something like `private-data.csv` inside the `data` folder.

Check that `data/private-data.csv` does not appear in `git status`.

Now add and commit `.gitignore` itself:

```bash
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add gitignore"
git push
```

## Step 10: Clone Into a Second Directory

Move back out of the folder and clone the repository somewhere else:

```bash
cd ..
git clone https://github.com/[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]/[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME].git [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]-copy
cd [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]-copy
```

## Step 11: Make a Change in the Copy

In your editor, create a new file named `notes.md`.

Type one line of text and save it.

```bash
git add notes.md
git commit -m "Add notes file"
git push
```

## Step 12: Pull the Change Into the Original Directory

```bash
cd ../[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
git pull
```

Confirm that the new file is now present.

## AI Tip

AI tools are really good at Git.

Good uses include:

1. asking what a command does
2. asking how to fix a common Git error
3. asking what sequence of commands you need next


## Status

You should end with:

1. a local repository
2. at least two commits
3. a remote GitHub repository
4. one ignored file or directory
5. a successful push and pull workflow across two local copies

## Authentication for Private Repositories

For a private repository, GitHub will require authentication when you push, pull, or clone.

Common options are:

1. Sign in through your browser when Git prompts you.
2. Use a personal access token instead of a password.
3. Set up SSH keys and use an SSH remote URL.

If GitHub Desktop, VS Code, or the GitHub CLI is installed, they can often handle authentication for you.
